In the angular surroundings of BaySixty6, beneath the busy Westway flyover,Sky Brownhas just finished pulling all kinds of skate tricks forES Magazine’sphotographer. Now, dressed down in cargo trousers and a Nike hoodie, she’s ready to chill. But first she needs to pee. The UK’s youngest-ever Olympic medallist is faced with a choice: there are no women’s toilets, only men’s and a disabled cubicle. She opts for the disabled. The most exciting female in British skating is on her home turf, and somehow she’s still out of place.

Feeling out of place, though, is Sky Brown’s homeostasis. Her life is lined with the stretch marks of being first in her field. In headlines, her name is nearly always followed by the phrase ‘youngest ever’, from taking part in the Vans US Open aged eight, to earning a Nike sponsorship aged 11, to qualifying for the Tokyo Olympics aged 13. Her most recent accolade was a gold medal at the 2023 WorldSkateboardingChampionships, making her the first British world champion in history. She rubs shoulders with the greats, texting Avril Lavigne and training with snowboarder Shaun White. In 2021, skateboarding legend Tony Hawk described Sky as ‘one of the best female skaters ever, if not one of the best wellrounded skaters ever, regardless of gender’.

Despite her highly decorated status and increasingly recognisable profile, Sky, now 15, says she still gets ‘the look’ from boys at the skate park. ‘It’s like, “Why is she here? Why is this little girl here?”’ she explains, sitting cross-legged in front of me in the skate shop of BaySixty6. ‘I’ve been around [skating] since I was really tiny, I was this little girl showing up at the skate park. And of course these big boys are going to look at me, but honestly — I just want to show them what’s up. If they give me that look, it just makes me pumped up.’

To be fair, when the world was first introduced to the always-smiling, relentlessly optimistic force of nature that is Sky Brown, she was still a little girl. Many discovered her in 2019, when the then-10-year-old announced that she would be joining Great Britain for the Tokyo 2020 Olympics. Taught to skate by her father, Stuart, at a young age, Sky forced her way on to the board more than she found it. ‘I watched him and stole his board,’ Sky laughs, ‘he didn’t want me to because that was his thing. But I still ended up stealing it.’ When young, skate-obsessed Sky wouldn’t give up, so Stuart decided he might as well coach her. And anything he couldn’t teach, she learnt through YouTube — when she wasn’t busy watching make-up tutorials like any of her peers.

Now she beams just as brightly as she when she was 10, but there’s something else at play, too. ‘I have more fire in me than ever,’ she says confidently. This becomes clear when I probe her about those boys who give her ‘the look’. ‘I like to show them my power — like, I like to go high, higher than the boys,’ she grins. ‘I like to grind longer than the boys... tweak it harder than the boys. And because my style is making it look beautiful, to make it look prettier than the boys.’

Sky’s decision to join Team GB in 2019 came as a slight surprise: up until this point, it was assumed she would be opting for the Japanese team. Her father is British, but her mother, Meiko, is Japanese and Sky grew up primarily between Miyazaki, on the Japanese island of Kyushu, and California. Despite the lack of the Queen’s English, she insists that she’s British in her own small ways: she loves a curry, a pie and a fry-up, and she’s even recently come around to the concept of baked beans. ‘[Britain] definitely feels like one of my homes now,’ she says. ‘I come here a lot and every time I do it feels like home.’ She’s not quite up-to-date on the slang — peng and clapped leave her puzzled — but she does occasionally throw a wholly authentic ‘gosh’ into conversation.

Sky took home a bronze medal for Great Britain at the Tokyo Olympics in 2021 at the age of 13. It felt like a miracle, not because her skills weren’t up to scratch but because she had faced a life-threatening injury the year before that nearly stopped her from skating completely. In May 2020, just over one year before the Covid-postponed Olympics, Sky was training in one of Tony Hawk’s skating gyms when she failed to land a jump between two half-pipe ramps. In a video posted on her YouTube channel, you can see the moment the board leaves Sky’s feet and she free falls 15 feet on to the ground, fracturing her skull and breaking bones in her arm.

It is a tough watch for anyone, let alone someone close to Sky. Her family didn’t want her to skate any more. They didn’t even want her to watch the video, let alone post it online. But she thought it looked cool, and she stands by that. ‘Oh definitely, I still think it looks cool. It’s part of my story. Looking back, it definitely was a scary moment and… it hurted!’ she jokes, a reference to an old skateboarding Vine video from seven years ago. ‘But it just made me stronger, it made me who I am today.’

Convincing her family to let her back on the board wasn’t easy. Sky’s mother completely lost the ability to watch her skate live, and had to instead watch replays of her competitions. ‘She was definitely having a hard time,’ Sky says of her mum. ‘I mean, she’s still scared to watch me but she will cheer me on, always.’ Sky’s father, Stuart, had less of a leg to stand on because he was the person who got her into skating in the first place. So when Sky wanted to get straight back on the board — ‘It was pretty easy,’ she says nonchalantly — Stuart had no choice but to continue what he’d started. The pair went back into training and secured Sky her bronze medal. Between Sky’s comeback, her position as a girl in a male dominated sport and the fact Tokyo was the first Olympics to include skateboarding in the history of the games, Sky’s fame started to grow.

For someone so young, you might think this would be a bit much: especially when, following on from the Olympics, her celebrity reached a level at which people were finding out where she lived and leaving gifts and cards outside her California home, prompting the family to move. Her hobby officially became her work. At this point, she says, she’s done more interviews and photo shoots than she can count. I ask her for a ballpark figure. She genuinely can’t name one. But the joy is still there. The language she uses around her professional sport is still that of fun: the board is her ‘toy’, the skate park is her ‘playground’. It definitely doesn’t feel like work, she says. ‘I just want to put on a show. I want to make people excited to watch me, to see the beauty in skating.’

In fact, she only wants to do more. This year, Sky entered for both skateboarding and surfing at theParis Olympics, and was clear on stating her hopes to win a medal in both. She sadly failed to qualify for surfing, missing out by one place, but isn’t giving up any time soon. ‘It was a little bit of a bummer,’ she says, ‘but I’m always going to be surfing.’ When I ask her what she’d be if she weren’t an athlete, she tells me assuredly she ‘would have always found [her] way into the water’.

For Sky, it’s a way of life. Her speech is littered with skate terminology and slang, and during our photo shoot she takes every opportunity to get her feet off the cold English concrete and on to her board. She’s also been imbued with a love of skate culture. Sure, she might be a little young for the likes of Kids or Street Dreams but she was raised watching Lords of Dogtown and listening to pop-punk queen Avril Lavigne, after whom Stuart jokes they almost named Sky. Even though Lavigne’s hit ‘Sk8er Boi’ was released six years before Sky was even born, she still sees her as a skating icon to this day. ‘She is one of my idols,’ Sky says, ‘she’s really sweet, we’re still talking and she sent me a signed board that says “skater girl”. I’m so thankful to have that friendship with her.’

It’s not her only impressive deck insignia, either. After becoming TAG Heuer’s youngest brand ambassador at the age of 14, Sky gets to rep the legendary luxury watchmaker’s logo on the bottom of her board, and utilises its collection of impressive timepieces to clock her runs. Last week, the watch brand set up a floating half-pipe nestled between two double-decker buses at the foot of Tower Bridge for Sky to show off her moves.

And it’s all in the name of practice for Paris, which Sky promises will be quite the spectacle. ‘I can’t wait,’ she says. ‘We’ve grown so much since Tokyo it’s actually insane. The women’s level has been growing, there’s so many new people — it’s going to be the best show ever.’

There’s that trademark Sky Brown optimism. But what about a medal? ‘Oh, I’m definitely going for gold,’ she says, more fiery than ever.

title: Sky Brown interview: 'The Olympics? I’m definitely going for gold' | The Standard
author: Maddy Mussen
description: ‘Why is that little girl here?’ It’s a phrase Sky Brown has had to live with in the male world of skate parks. But the 15-year-old will put an end to that if she gets her way in Paris, she tells Maddy Mussen
